signals: More than 70% of adults treated for primary hypertension will eventually require at least two antihypertensive agents
combination therapy for the treatment of hypertension
stepwise therapy with a beta blocker and an ACEI or ARB, followed by add-on therapy
Treatment for patients with chronic kidney disease

extraction.summary: More than 70% of adults with primary hypertension require combination therapy, typically using thiazide diuretics, calcium channel blockers, ACEIs, or ARBs. Combination therapy is indicated when monotherapy fails to achieve adequate control or for initial therapy in patients with severely elevated blood pressure (systolic >160 mmHg or >20 mmHg above goal).

population.disease_definition: Blood pressure above guideline thresholds requiring pharmaceutical intervention
population.severity_staging: Based on blood pressure thresholds: JNC8 (≥140/90 for adults <60, ≥150/90 for adults ≥60), ACC/AHA (≥130/80), ESC varies by age and comorbidities

key_thresholds.context: Threshold for considering loop diuretic instead of thiazide
More intensive BP control in CKD patients showed mortality benefit vs 8 mmHg reduction
Demonstrated reduction in micro/macrovascular events in diabetes patients

recommendations.recommendation: For most patients, combination therapy should include an ACEI or ARB, thiazide diuretic, or calcium channel blocker
recommendations.notes: Consistent evidence from RCTs showing reduced morbidity and mortality

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with chronic kidney disease and proteinuria should be prescribed an ACEI or ARB as part of combination therapy
recommendations.notes: Consistent evidence from RCTs showing reduced morbidity and mortality

recommendations.recommendation: The combination of an ACEI and an ARB should be avoided
recommendations.notes: RCT showed benefit is outweighed by increased morbidity

recommendations.recommendation: In black patients, at least one agent should be a thiazide diuretic or calcium channel blocker
Initial combination therapy achieves blood pressure control more quickly than monotherapy
Therapy escalation should occur within one month if needed to achieve target blood pressure
Use ACEI or ARB in patients with chronic kidney disease, in addition to thiazide diuretic or CCB
Use ACEIs or ARBs in patients with proteinuria, including those with diabetes
recommendations.notes: Reduces proteinuria and progression to ESRD

recommendations.recommendation: Do not combine ACEI and ARB
recommendations.notes: Increased risk of ESRD without mortality benefit

recommendations.recommendation: Consider loop diuretic if eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m²
recommendations.notes: Based on low-quality evidence that thiazides have decreased effectiveness

recommendations.recommendation: Use fixed-dose combination of ACEI and thiazide diuretic in adults with diabetes
recommendations.notes: ACCOMPLISH trial showed reduced micro/macrovascular events

medications.indication: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction

red_flags: Blood pressure not controlled with maximal triple therapy
Volume overload in heart failure patients
Contraindications to beta blockers in heart failure
Therapeutic inertia - failure to escalate therapy within one month
Use of ACEI and ARB combination
Adverse events from ACEI/ARB combination therapy
extraction.follow_up: Escalation of therapy should occur within one month if blood pressure targets are not achieved. Continue monitoring until blood pressure goal is reached, then routine follow-up per standard hypertension management.
extraction.escalation_path: If not at blood pressure goal on maximal dosages of triple therapy, add additional medication classes or refer to specialist. Consider disease-specific combinations for patients with heart failure, chronic kidney disease with proteinuria, or diabetes mellitus.
